note:
Accel 领投了这轮融资，其中还包括 SignalFire 的参与，并使这家位于山景城但完全分布式的初创公司自 2019 年成立以来的总资金达到 550 万美元。联合创始人兼首席执行官 Val Agostino 是创建 Mint.com 的原始团队的第一位产品经理。他说，在那里，他亲眼目睹了对财务问题有更深入了解的美国人“需要不仅仅是跟踪和预算的软件解决方案”。 “他们需要帮助规划他们的财务未来并了解相互竞争的财务优先事项之间的权衡，”他说。 Monarch 旨在通过软件帮助人们满足这些需求，它说“让人们可以轻松”勾勒出他们的财务目标，然后制定详细的、前瞻性的计划来实现这些目标。 “然后，我们帮助客户根据他们的计划跟踪他们的进度，并在他们的财务状况发生变化时自动纠正路线，这总是如此，”Agostino 说。 Monarch 于 2021 年初推出了适用于网络、iOS 和 Android 的应用程序的内测，定价为每月 9.99 美元或每年 89.99 美元。这家初创公司故意选择不接受广告支持或出售客户的财务数据。 “我们认为订阅商业模式最能支持这种精神，并使我们用户的兴趣与我们自己的兴趣保持一致，”他补充道。自公开发布以来，Monarch 的付费用户群每周增长约 9%。
阿戈斯蒂诺认为，Monarch 在大流行期间推出，其不确定性影响了人们的财务生活。 “因此，我们看到很多人利用 Monarch 的预测功能来比较不同的‘假设’情景，例如换工作或搬到不同的城市或州，”他说。本月早些时候，TechCrunch 报道了一家具有类似使命的公司 BodesWell 与美国运通合作为其持卡人提供财务规划工具。 Agostino 表示，Monarch 与 BodesWell 的相似之处在于，这两家初创公司都帮助客户制定财务计划和未来。 “不同之处在于 Monarch 还拥有一整套 PFM 工具，例如预算、报告和投资分析，”他说。 “对消费者的好处是，因为 Monarch 与您的整个财务状况相关联，我们可以帮助您真正按照财务计划进行和/或在需要时实时更新计划。” Accel 的 Daniel Levine 说，在他遇到 Monarch 之前，他“尽管已经过时了，但不知何故仍然是 Mint 的客户。”莱文说，在过去十年中，金融产品的格局急剧扩大，例如，越来越多的人拥有经纪和加密账户。在他看来，Monarch 脱颖而出有几个原因。一方面，它是一种订阅产品。
“我一直讨厌 Mint 的一件事是它会为我推荐客观上错误的信用卡，”莱文说。 “它有我所有的交易数据，它应该告诉我对我来说奖励最好的卡。 Monarch 的宗旨是永远不会为了广告而牺牲对用户最有利的东西。”其次，Monarch 的目标是作为其客户的基础设施。为此，它需要监控某人的所有财务状况。 “他们需要跟踪支票、信用卡、经纪、房地产和加密货币，”他说。 “君主致力于这样做。这是一个令人难以置信的痛苦问题，尽管 Monarch 是该领域的新进入者，但我认为他们在那个维度上显然已经分开了。”
note_en:
Accel led the round, which also included participation from SignalFire, and brings the Mountain View-based yet fully distributed startup’s total funding since its 2019 inception to $5.5 million.
 Co-founder and CEO Val Agostino was the first product manager on the original team that built Mint.com. There, he said, he saw firsthand that Americans with a greater understanding of financial matters “needed software solutions that went beyond just tracking and budgeting.”
  “They needed help planning their financial future and understanding the tradeoffs between competing financial priorities,” he said.
 Monarch aims to help people address those needs with software it says “makes it easy” for people to outline their financial goals and then create a detailed, forward-looking plan toward achieving them.
 “We then help customers track their progress against their plan and automatically course correct as their financial situation changes, which it always does,” Agostino said.
 Monarch came out of private beta in early 2021 with apps for web, iOS and Android, and is priced at $9.99 per month or $89.99 per year. The startup intentionally opted to not be ad-supported or sell customers’ financial data.
  “We felt that a subscription business model would best support that ethos and align our users’ interests with our own,” he added. Since launching publicly, Monarch has been growing its paid subscriber base by about 9% per week.
  Monarch launched during the pandemic, the uncertainty of which carried over into people’s financial lives, believes Agostino.
 “As a result, we saw a lot of people make use of Monarch’s forecasting features to compare different ‘what if ‘scenarios such as switching jobs or moving to a different city or state,” he said.
 Earlier this month, TechCrunch reported on a company with a similar mission,  BodesWell, teaming up with American Express on a financial planning tool for its cardholders. Agostino said that  Monarch is similar to BodesWell in that both startups help customers map a financial plan and future.
  “The difference is that Monarch also has a full suite of PFM tools, such as budgeting, reporting and investment analysis,” he said. “The benefit to the consumer is that because Monarch is connected to your entire financial picture, we can help you actually stay on track with your financial plan and/or update the plan in real time if needed.”
 Accel’s Daniel Levine said that until he came across Monarch, he was “ somehow still a Mint customer despite its obsolescence.”
 Over the past decade, the landscape for financial products has expanded dramatically, with more people having brokerage and crypto accounts, for example, Levine said.
 In his view, Monarch stands out for a couple of reasons. For one, it’s a subscription product.
 “One thing I always hated about Mint was when it would suggest the objectively wrong credit card for me,” Levine said. “It has all of my transaction data, it should tell me the card with the best rewards for me. Monarch is set up to never compromise what’s best for the user in favor of advertising.”
 Secondly, Monarch’s aim is to serve as the infrastructure for its customers. To do that, it needs to monitor all of someone’s finances.
 “They need to track checking, credit cards, brokerage, real estate and crypto,” he said. “Monarch is committed to doing that. It’s an incredibly painful problem and even though Monarch is a new entrant in the space, I think they’ve clearly separated themselves on that dimension.”
title: Mint 的第一位 PM 为 Accel 支持的资金管理平台 Monarch 筹集了数百万美元
title_en: Mint’s first PM raises millions for Monarch, an Accel-backed money management platform

note:
Snap 于 2017 年以 240 亿美元的估值上市，但不久之前，这款短暂的照片共享应用程序遇到了巨大的麻烦：Instagram 克隆了他们当时独一无二的 Stories 功能。 Instagram Stories 推出后，Snapchat 的增长放缓了 82%。然后，当 Snapchat 重新设计其应用程序界面时，Kylie Jenner 在推特上表示她不再使用该应用程序，导致该公司的估值下降了 12 亿美元。 sooo 有没有其他人不再打开 Snapchat 了？或者只是我……呃，这太可悲了。但 Snapchat 坚持下来并卷土重来。其收入在 2020 年第四季度创下 9.11 亿美元的历史新高，然后在下个季度下降至 7.7 亿美元。现在，Snapchat 在 2021 年第二季度的收入超过了之前的高点，达到 9.82 亿美元。该应用程序的第二季度增长可归因于在大流行高峰期间缩减支出的广告商的回归，以及在锁定期间涌向该应用程序的用户的保留。与许多社交媒体平台一样，Snapchat 在大流行期间增加了收入和用户群，但这不仅仅是让用户重新使用他们成长起来的应用程序的问题。随着 TikTok 在现场爆炸式增长和创作者经济蓬勃发展，Snapchat 通过创建 Spotlight（TikTok 克隆版）和投资增强现实应用来跟上步伐。 “本季度我们在增强现实平台方面取得了重大进展，”斯皮格尔说。 “平均每天有超过 2 亿 Snapchatter 使用 AR，超过 200,000 名创作者使用 Lens Studio 为我们的社区构建 AR 镜头。”上个月，Snapchat 因其卡通 3D 风格镜头而走红，让你看起来像皮克斯电影中的角色。斯皮格尔特别提到这个镜头是“突出了镜头在 Snapchat 内外传播的力量”的功能。但除了有趣的面部过滤器，Snapchat 一直在使用 AR 来吸引电子商务合作伙伴。该应用程序为沃尔特迪斯尼世界、Smile Direct Club、Zenni Optical、elf Cosmetics、Ralph Lauren 等开发了 AR 体验。这包括手表、珠宝、眼镜、手袋、化妆品甚至服装的试穿功能。在 5 月份的合作伙伴峰会上，Snapchat 公布了一项更新，让用户可以扫描朋友的服装，以找到相似款式的购物推荐。 Snap 首席商务官 Jeremi Gorman 表示：“我们还有很多工作要做来构建我们的技术并提高 AR 的采用率，但我们对合作伙伴在我们投资于我们的长期相机机会时所看到的结果感到非常兴奋. “我们对我们的长期机会充满信心，并很高兴通过增强现实在购物和商业上加倍努力。”
今年 3 月，Snap 收购了 Fit Analytics，这是一家总部位于柏林的初创公司，可帮助购物者在网上购物时找到合适尺码的服装和鞋类。结合 Snap 在 AR 上的投入，我们最终能不能用 AR 来看看订购什么尺码的衣服？此类技术的应用需要谨慎处理，尤其是在青少年饮食失调的比率呈上升趋势的情况下。除了电子商务，Snapchat 还寻求与 HBO Max 和环球音乐集团等娱乐公司建立战略合作伙伴关系，并在其 Spectacles 眼镜上加倍投入，这种眼镜可以创造 AR 体验。当然，Facebook 也在研发 AR 眼镜。但对于两家公司来说，Snap 最近的成功表明 AR 体验的采用率和价值正在上升。
note_en:
Snap went public in 2017 with a  $24 billion valuation, but not long before then, the ephemeral photo sharing app experienced a massive hiccup: Instagram cloned their then-unique Stories feature. After Instagram Stories launched, Snapchat’s  growth slowed by 82%. Then, when Snapchat redesigned its app’s interface, Kylie Jenner  tweeted that she didn’t use the app anymore, causing the company’s valuation to  drop by $1.2 billion.
 sooo does anyone else not open Snapchat anymore? Or is it just me… ugh this is so sad.
 But Snapchat held on and made a  comeback. Its revenue reached an all-time high of  $911 million in Q4 of 2020, then went down to  $770 million the following quarter. Now, Snapchat’s revenue in Q2 of 2021 surpasses its previous high to reach  $982 million.
 The app’s Q2 growth could be attributed to the return of advertisers who scaled back their spending during the height of the pandemic, as well as the retention of users that flocked to the app while in lockdown. Like many social media platforms, Snapchat  grew its revenue and user base during the pandemic, but this isn’t just a matter of re-engaging users with an app that they grew out of. As TikTok exploded on the scene and the  creator economy boomed, Snapchat kept up by creating  Spotlight, a TikTok clone, and investing in the applications of augmented reality.
 “We made significant progress with our augmented reality platform this quarter,” Spiegel said. “More than 200 million Snapchatters engage with AR every day on average, and over 200,000 creators use Lens Studio to build AR Lenses for our community.”
 Last month, Snapchat went viral for its  Cartoon 3D Style Lens, which makes you look like a character in a Pixar movie. Spiegel specifically mentioned this lens as a feature that “highlighted the power of Lenses to go viral both inside and outside of Snapchat.” But beyond fun face filters, Snapchat has been using AR to woo ecommerce partners. The app has developed AR experiences for Walt Disney World, Smile Direct Club, Zenni Optical, e.l.f. Cosmetics, Ralph Lauren, and more. This includes try-on capabilities for watches, jewelry, eyewear, handbags, makeup, and even clothing. At its Partner Summit in May, Snapchat revealed an update that lets users  scan friends’ outfits to find shopping recommendations for similar styles.
 “We have a lot more work ahead to build out our technology and increase AR adoption, but we are thrilled with the results that our partners are seeing as we invest in our long-term camera opportunity,” said Jeremi Gorman, Snap’s Chief Business Officer. “We are confident in our long-term opportunity, and are excited to double down on shopping and commerce via augmented reality.”
 In March, Snap acquired  Fit Analytics, a Berlin-based startup that helps shoppers find the right-sized apparel and footwear when shopping online. Combined with Snap’s investment in AR, could we eventually use AR to see which size of clothing to order? The application of that sort of technology would need to be handled sensitively, especially as the rates of eating disorders in teens are  on the rise.
 Beyond ecommerce, Snapchat has sought out strategic partnerships with entertainment companies like  HBO Max and  Universal Music Group and doubled down on its  Spectacles, glasses that create AR experiences. Of course, Facebook is working on  AR glasses too. But for both companies, Snap’s recent successes show the rising adoption and value of AR experiences.
title: Snap 创四年来最佳季度业绩
title_en: Snap had its best quarter in four years

note: 由于潜在的火灾问题，通用汽车正在对 2017 年至 2019 年的 Bolt 电动汽车进行第二次召回。该公司表示，它计划更换有缺陷的电池，但在此之前，它建议 Bolt 客户将充电量限制在 90%，并且不要低于 70 英里的范围。它还重申了上周的建议，反对将车停在室内，并让汽车在无人看管的情况下过夜充电。这次最新的召回是在去年 11 月的一次类似召回之后进行的，当时通用汽车召回了 68,000 多个螺栓。该公司还建议 Bolt 客户访问离他们最近的雪佛兰 EV 经销商以获取高级诊断软件，该软件应在未来出现任何电池问题之前提醒他们。现代汽车也像通用汽车一样从 LG Chem 采购电池，最终为其 Kona EV 更换了 75,000 多个电池。虽然听起来可能令人震惊——通用汽车的召回是由 2017 年至 2019 年期间的五起 Bolt 火灾引发的——但值得注意的是，根据 FEMA 的一份报告，汽油车通常每天会引起大约 150 起火灾。尽管如此，电动汽车制造商仍需要证明他们能够负责任地处理潜在问题，以免伤害更多人（以及导致人们对电动汽车产生更多负面情绪）。
note_en:
GM is  issuing a second recall for 2017 to 2019 Bolt EVs over potential fire issues. The company says it plans to replace defective batteries, but until it can do so it’s advising Bolt customers to limit their charging up to 90 percent, and not to go below 70 miles of range. It’s also  reiterating a recommendation from last week against parking indoors and leaving the car’s to charge overnight unattended. This latest recall follows a similar one from last November, where  GM recalled more than 68,000 Bolts.
 The company also suggests that Bolt customers visit their nearest Chevy EV dealer to get the  advanced diagnostics software, which should alert them ahead of any future battery issues. Hyundai, which also sources batteries from LG Chem like GM, ended up replacing more than 75,000 batteries for its Kona EV.
 While it may sound alarming — GM’s recalls were triggered by five Bolt fires between 2017 and 2019 — it’s worth noting that gas cars typically cause around 150 fires a day,  according to a FEMA report. Still, EV makers need to prove they can responsibly deal with potential issues before they can hurt more people (and before it leads to more negative sentiment towards electric vehicles).
title: 通用汽车因火灾风险再次召回 Bolt EV
title_en: GM recalls Bolt EVs once again over fire risks

note:
科学中最重要的开放性问题之一是我们的意识是如何建立的。在 1990 年代，早在因对黑洞的预测而获得 2020 年诺贝尔物理学奖之前，物理学家罗杰彭罗斯与麻醉学家斯图尔特哈默罗夫合作提出了一个雄心勃勃的答案。他们声称大脑的神经元系统形成了一个复杂的网络，由此产生的意识应该遵守量子力学的规则——该理论决定了像电子这样的微小粒子如何移动。他们认为，这可以解释人类意识的神秘复杂性。彭罗斯和哈梅洛夫遭到了怀疑。量子力学定律通常只适用于非常低的温度。例如，量子计算机目前在 -272°C 左右运行。在更高的温度下，经典力学接管了。由于我们的身体在室温下工作，您会期望它受经典物理定律的支配。出于这个原因，量子意识理论已被许多科学家彻底驳回——尽管其他人被说服了支持者。我没有参与这场辩论，而是决定与上海交通大学金贤敏教授领导的中国同事联手，测试支撑量子意识理论的一些原则。在我们的新论文中，我们研究了量子粒子如何在像大脑这样的复杂结构中移动——但在实验室环境中。如果有一天我们的发现可以与大脑中测量的活动进行比较，我们可能离验证或驳回彭罗斯和哈默洛夫有争议的理论又近了一步。我们的大脑由称为神经元的细胞组成，它们的综合活动被认为会产生意识。每个神经元都包含微管，将物质运输到细胞的不同部分。 Penrose-Hameroff 量子意识理论认为，微管以分形模式构建，这将使量子过程发生。
分形是既不是二维也不是三维的结构，而是介于两者之间的一些分数值。在数学中，分形以美丽的图案出现，无限重复，产生看似不可能的东西：面积有限但周长无限的结构。这听起来可能无法想象，但分形实际上在自然界中经常发生。如果您仔细观察花椰菜的小花或蕨类植物的枝条，您会发现它们都由相同的基本形状组成，一遍又一遍地重复自己，但规模越来越小。这是分形的一个关键特征。如果您观察自己的身体内部，也会发生同样的情况：例如，您的肺部结构是分形的，循环系统中的血管也是如此。分形也出现在 MC Escher 和 Jackson Pollock 迷人的重复艺术作品中，并且它们已经在技术中使用了几十年，例如在天线设计中。这些都是经典分形的例子——遵循经典物理学而不是量子物理学定律的分形。很容易理解为什么分形被用来解释人类意识的复杂性。因为它们无限复杂，允许从简单重复的模式中出现复杂性，它们可能是支持我们思想神秘深处的结构。但如果是这种情况，它只能发生在量子水平上，微小粒子在大脑神经元内以分形模式移动。这就是为什么彭罗斯和哈默洛夫的提议被称为“量子意识”理论。我们还不能测量大脑中量子分形的行为——如果它们存在的话。但是先进的技术意味着我们现在可以在实验室中测量量子分形。在最近的一项涉及扫描隧道显微镜 (STM) 的研究中，我和我在乌得勒支的同事们小心翼翼地将电子排列成分形图案，创造出一个量子分形。
然后，当我们测量电子的波函数（描述它们的量子状态）时，我们发现它们也生活在由我们制作的物理模式决定的分形维数上。在这种情况下，我们在量子尺度上使用的模式是谢尔宾斯基三角形，它是一种介于一维和二维之间的形状。这是一个令人兴奋的发现，但 STM 技术无法探测量子粒子如何运动——这将告诉我们更多关于量子过程如何在大脑中发生的信息。所以在我们最新的研究中，我和上海交通大学的同事更进了一步。使用最先进的光子学实验，我们能够以前所未有的细节揭示分形内发生的量子运动。我们通过将光子（光粒子）注入人工芯片来实现这一目标，该芯片精心设计成一个微小的谢尔宾斯基三角形。我们在三角形的尖端注入光子，并观察它们如何在称为量子传输的过程中在其分形结构中传播。然后我们在两种不同的分形结构上重复了这个实验，它们的形状都是正方形而不是三角形。在这些结构中的每一个中，我们都进行了数百次实验。我们从这些实验中观察到的结果表明，量子分形实际上与经典分形表现不同。具体来说，我们发现与经典情况相比，在量子情况下，光在分形上的传播受不同定律的支配。这种关于量子分形的新知识可以为科学家们提供实验测试量子意识理论的基础。如果有一天从人脑中进行量子测量，它们可以与我们的结果进行比较，以确定意识是经典现象还是量子现象。我们的工作也可能对整个科学领域产生深远的影响。通过研究我们人工设计的分形结构中的量子传输，我们可能已经迈出了统一物理学、数学和生物学的第一步，这将极大地丰富我们对周围世界以及存在于我们脑海中的世界的理解.
本文根据知识共享许可从 The Conversation 重新发布。阅读原文。
note_en:
One of the most important open questions in science is how our  consciousness is established. In the 1990s,  long before winning the 2020 Nobel Prize in Physics for his prediction of  black holes, physicist Roger Penrose teamed up with anesthesiologist Stuart Hameroff to propose an ambitious answer.
   They claimed that the brain's neuronal system forms an intricate network and that the consciousness this produces should obey the rules of  quantum mechanics – the theory that determines how tiny particles like electrons move around. This, they argue, could explain the mysterious complexity of human consciousness.
 Penrose and Hameroff were met with incredulity. Quantum mechanical laws are usually only found to apply at  very low temperatures.  Quantum computers, for example, currently operate at around  -272°C. At higher temperatures, classical mechanics takes over.
 Since our body works at room temperature, you would expect it to be governed by the classical laws of physics. For this reason, the quantum consciousness theory has been  dismissed outright by many scientists – though others are  persuaded supporters.
 Instead of entering into this debate, I decided to join forces with colleagues from China, led by Professor Xian-Min Jin at Shanghai Jiaotong University, to test some of the principles underpinning the quantum theory of consciousness.
 In  our new paper, we've investigated how quantum particles could move in a complex structure like the brain – but in a lab setting. If our findings can one day be compared with activity measured in the brain, we may come one step closer to validating or dismissing Penrose and Hameroff's  controversial theory.
    Our brains are composed of cells called neurons, and their combined activity is believed to generate consciousness. Each neuron contains  microtubules, which transport substances to different parts of the cell. The Penrose-Hameroff theory of quantum consciousness argues that microtubules are structured in a  fractal pattern which would enable quantum processes to occur.
 Fractals are structures that are neither two-dimensional nor three-dimensional, but are instead some fractional value in between. In mathematics, fractals emerge as  beautiful patterns that repeat themselves infinitely, generating what is seemingly impossible: a structure that has a finite area, but an infinite perimeter.
 This might sound impossible to visualize, but fractals actually occur frequently  in nature. If you look closely at the florets  of a cauliflower or the branches  of a fern, you'll see that they're both made up of the same basic shape repeating itself over and over again, but at smaller and smaller scales. That's a key characteristic of fractals.
 The same happens if you look inside your own body: the structure of  your lungs, for instance, is fractal, as are the  blood vessels in your circulatory system. Fractals also feature in the enchanting repeating artworks of  MC Escher and  Jackson Pollock, and they've been used for decades in technology, such as in the  design of antennas.
 These are all examples of classical fractals – fractals that abide by the laws of classical physics rather than quantum physics.
  It's easy to see why fractals have been used to explain the complexity of human consciousness. Because they're infinitely intricate, allowing complexity to emerge from simple repeated patterns, they could be the structures that support the mysterious depths of our minds.
 But if this is the case, it could only be happening on the quantum level, with tiny particles moving in fractal patterns within the brain's neurons. That's why Penrose and Hameroff's proposal is called a theory of "quantum consciousness".
    We're not yet able to measure the behavior of quantum fractals in the brain – if they exist at all. But advanced technology means we can now measure quantum fractals in the lab. In  recent research involving a  scanning tunneling microscope (STM), my colleagues at Utrecht and I carefully arranged electrons in a fractal pattern, creating a quantum fractal.
 When we then measured the wave function of the electrons, which describes their quantum state, we found that they too lived at the fractal dimension dictated by the physical pattern we'd made. In this case, the pattern we used on the quantum scale was the  Sierpiński triangle, which is a shape that's somewhere between one-dimensional and two-dimensional.
  This was an exciting finding, but STM techniques cannot probe how quantum particles move – which would tell us more about how quantum processes might occur in the brain. So in  our latest research, my colleagues at Shanghai Jiaotong University and I went one step further. Using state-of-the-art photonics experiments, we were able to reveal the quantum motion that takes place within fractals in unprecedented detail.
 We achieved this by  injecting photons (particles of light) into an artificial chip that was painstakingly engineered into a tiny Sierpiński triangle.
   We injected photons at the tip of the triangle and watched how they spread throughout its fractal structure in a process called  quantum transport. We then repeated this experiment on two different fractal structures, both shaped as squares rather than triangles. And in each of these structures we conducted hundreds of experiments.
 Our observations from these experiments reveal that quantum fractals actually behave in a different way to classical ones. Specifically, we found that the spread of light across a fractal is governed by different laws in the quantum case compared to the classical case.
 This new knowledge of quantum fractals could provide the foundations for scientists to experimentally test the theory of quantum consciousness. If quantum measurements are one day taken from the human brain, they could be compared against our results to definitely decide whether consciousness is a classical or a quantum phenomenon.
 Our work could also have profound implications across scientific fields. By investigating quantum transport in our artificially designed fractal structures, we may have taken the first tiny steps towards the unification of physics, mathematics and biology, which could greatly enrich our understanding of the world around us as well as the world that exists in our heads.
  This article is republished from  The Conversation under a Creative Commons license. Read the  original article.
title: 意识是否受量子物理学的束缚？我们越来越接近发现
title_en: Is Consciousness Bound by Quantum Physics? We're Getting Closer to Finding Out

今天，我有一些激动人心的消息想与更广泛的 HashiCorp 社区分享：我正在过渡到一个新角色，作为全职个人贡献者和 HashiCorp 执行团队。我于 2012 年创立 HashiCorp，担任 CEO 至 2016 年，然后成为我们的 CTO 之一直到今天。作为一名高管，经过近 10 年的学习和成长，我已经准备好并很高兴回到全职工程职位，并期待通过这个新角色产生有意义的影响。由于 HashiCorp 的成熟和我们现在拥有的优秀领导团队，这一变化已经规划了多年，现在成为可能。我觉得解释有关此更改的更多详细信息的最佳方法是简单地分享我写给我们员工的电子邮件，因此我将其包含在下面。在我追求我的新角色时，我期待着作为我们社区的一员继续与你们所有人交谈。从今天开始，我将在 HashiCorp 过渡到一个新角色，作为专注于工程的个人贡献者。这是我非常兴奋的事情，我和执行团队已经计划了很长时间。我想借此机会在这封电子邮件中填写有关此更改的背景信息，并描述这对我和公司未来的意义。自从创立 HashiCorp 以来，我一直认为建立一家不需要我进行日常运营并且其他领导者可以在正确阶段介入的公司很重要。我相信这对于一家公司具有弹性和持久性是必要的。这种心态最早可以在 2016 年看到，当时我和 Armon [Dadgar] 主动寻找一位 CEO 来接替我们并带领公司向前发展。在我们的搜索中，我们找到了 Dave [McJannet]，他曾经是并将继续是公司下一阶段的正确领导者。那时，我担任了 CTO 的新角色，这是过去几年更适合我的角色。这个决定也有个人因素。我作为一名对基础设施工具充满热情的工程师创立了 HashiCorp。但作为创始人，我的角色有时不得不远远超出或远离。这就是成为创始人的代价：你做任何需要你做的事情，即使这个角色的某些部分并没有特别激励你。在将 HashiCorp 打造为一家价值数十亿美元的公司近十年的过程中，我不断重申我仍然是一名工程师，我准备更正式地重新专注于这一点。 HashiCorp 再次让我感受到公司的存在，我已经准备好迎接新的角色。 HashiCorp 的领导团队很强大，我完全信任他们。业务很健康，我对未来充满乐观。
在我的新角色中，我将成为特定产品团队的个人贡献者或探索 HashiCorp 内的其他想法。我将继续领导某些大局规划，例如主要产品计划、HashiConf 主题演讲的消息传递和其他战略决策。我将继续成为 HashiCorp 的全职员工。然而，当我进入这个角色时，我将正式不再是执行团队的成员，因此将不再有机会参加执行会议、计划或决策。恰逢我的角色转变，我也从 HashiCorp 董事会辞职。虽然我们决定匹配时间，但我想强调的是，这是我们多年前做出的决定，与我不断变化的角色完全无关。随着初创公司进入后期阶段，预计董事会将拥有更多独立成员，并且通常只有一位创始人与 CEO 一起进入后期董事会。我完全信任 Armon 和 Dave 代表员工，包括我自己。虽然我将不再担任执行团队或董事会成员，但我仍将是一名充满热情和积极性的 HashiCorp 员工和工程负责人。我很高兴能够将更多时间投入到产品和工程挑战上，并将更多的日常时间花在直接与这些组织的成员一起工作上。我的经验、声音和专业知识对公司的每个人来说都是完全可用的。我非常自豪，作为一名高管，我帮助 HashiCorp 从一无所有发展到近 1,500 名员工，估值超过 50 亿美元。展望未来，公司拥有巨大的机会，我很高兴能继续与大家合作，共同建设和发展公司，并对我的新角色产生影响。
note_en:
Today, I have some exciting news that I wanted to share with the broader HashiCorp community: I am transitioning into a new role as a full-time individual contributor and off of the HashiCorp executive team.
 I founded HashiCorp in 2012 and served as CEO until 2016, and then became one of our CTOs until today. After nearly 10 years of learning and growing as an executive, I'm ready and excited to step back into a full-time engineering position and look forward to making a meaningful impact with this new role. This change has been years in planning and is now possible thanks to HashiCorp’s maturity and the excellent leadership team we now have in place.
 I feel the best way to explain more details about this change is to simply share the email I wrote to our employees, so I’ve included that below. I look forward to continuing to talk with you all as part of our community as I pursue my new role.
   Beginning today, I'll be transitioning into a new role here at HashiCorp as an individual contributor focused on engineering. This is something I'm very excited about and that the executive team and I have planned for a long time. I'd like to take the opportunity in this email to fill in the background about this change and describe what this means for me and the company going forward.
 Ever since founding HashiCorp, I've felt it's important to build a company where I'm not required for day-to-day operations and where other leaders can step in at the right phase. I believe this is necessary for a company to be resilient and long-lasting. This mindset could be seen as early as 2016 when Armon [Dadgar] and I proactively sought out a CEO to succeed us and lead the company forward. In our search we found Dave [McJannet] and he was, and continues to be, the right leader for the next phases of the company. At that time, I stepped into a new role as CTO, one that has been better suited to me for the last few years.
 There are also personal elements to this decision. I founded HashiCorp as an engineer passionate about infrastructure tooling. But as a founder, my role at times has had to expand well beyond and away from that. That's the price of being a founder: you do whatever is necessary of you, even if there are parts of the role that don’t particularly motivate you. And over the course of nearly a decade building HashiCorp into a multi-billion dollar company, I've continuously reaffirmed that I'm still an engineer at heart and I'm ready to more officially get back to focusing on that.
 HashiCorp is once again at a place where I feel the company and I are ready for me to step into a new role. The HashiCorp leadership team is strong and I trust them completely. The business is healthy and I have complete optimism about the future.
 In my new role, I will be an individual contributor either on specific product teams or exploring other ideas within HashiCorp. I will continue to participate with leadership in certain big-picture planning such as major product plans, messaging for HashiConf keynotes, and other strategic decisions. I'll continue to be a full-time HashiCorp employee. However, as I step into this role, I will officially no longer be a member of the executive team and will therefore no longer have access to executive meetings, plans, or decisions.
 Coinciding with my role change, I am also stepping down from the HashiCorp board. While we've decided to match the timing, I'd like to stress that this was a decision we made years ago and is not related at all to my changing role. As a startup matures into a later stage, it is expected for the board to have more independent members and it is typical for only one founder to be on a late-stage board alongside the CEO. I fully trust Armon and Dave to represent the employees, myself included.
 While I will no longer be on the executive team or board, I will remain a passionate and active HashiCorp employee and engineering leader. I'm very excited to be able to dedicate more of my time towards product and engineering challenges and to spend more of my day-to-day time working directly alongside members in those organizations. My experience, voice, and expertise remain fully available to everyone in the company.
 I'm incredibly proud that as an executive, I helped HashiCorp grow from nothing to nearly 1,500 employees with a valuation of over $5 billion. Looking forward, the company has a huge opportunity, and I'm excited to continue working with you all to build and grow the company and make an impact in my new role.
title: Mitchell Hashimoto 在 HashiCorp 担任新的个人贡献者角色
title_en: Mitchell Hashimoto takes on a new individual contributor role at HashiCorp

我很头痛。不是低级的，烦人的，“我头疼”之类的头疼。我也明白这些。大多数人都这样做，他们是一个拖累。不，当我说我头疼时，我的意思是在很大程度上不可预测的时间间隔，我的脑海深处升起一个痛结，总是在我的右眼球后面感觉到。然后它迅速点击强度等级，跳过你盯着屏幕太久可能会感到的钝痛，跳过你兄弟婚礼后早上的那种昏昏欲睡的感觉，跳过痛苦但短暂的刺痛冰淇淋头疼，几分钟之内，疼痛如此剧烈和持续，以至于我只能抓住坚固的东西，来回晃动，咕哝，直到它消退。我的就是医生所说的“主要头痛”之一——反复出现且经常令人难以忍受的疾病，这些疾病不是另一种疾病的副产品（或昨晚的鸡尾酒自我造成的），而是无情的，而且在许多方面，他们自己对疾病的了解仍然很少。我们通过偏头痛等常见名称了解它们，它影响着数千万美国人，尤其是女性。我患有另一种称为丛集性头痛（技术上是“三叉自主神经性头痛”）的症状。还有其他一些，用无数且不完美的线条来区分它们。如果您经历过偏头痛或丛集性头痛——研究表明全世界有超过 10 亿人患有此病——那么您可能对在医生之间穿梭寻找“得到它”的人有所了解。你知道欣然吞下没有真正作用的药丸是什么感觉，而这会让你在其他方面很痛苦。你甚至可能知道那种让我怀疑的难以置信的愤怒，从我躺在浴室地板上的胎儿姿势来看：“科学怎么可能无法解决一个该死的头痛？”我不会说我们已经接近修复了。在许多方面，考虑到这些疾病在医疗保健费用中提取的数十亿美元，以及它们对人们造成的巨大损失——通常是在他们最有生产力和创造力的时候，我们离理解这些疾病还很远。年。但我也可以自信地说：可能是头疼的最佳时机。 3 月，神经科学领域最令人梦寐以求的奖项之一，150 万美元的大脑奖颁给了四位科学家，他们通常彼此独立工作，大约从 1980 年代开始，开始彻底改变我们对偏头痛和类似头痛的理解。他们和其他许多人的工作缓慢而稳定，推翻了长期盛行且轻率的概念，例如“偏头痛人格”（主要针对女性的嘲讽类别），或者压力和体质虚弱是原因的想法。他们不是。相反，原发性头痛是真正的神经生物学障碍，我知道这一点，因为在去年春天的一个阳光明媚的日子里，在用糟糕的药物不完全控制极度疼痛并与医生和保险公司讨价还价几周后，我站在厨房里，走下我的裤子，并在我的底部三针（仍然非常昂贵）注射了一种新的灵丹妙药——一种单克隆抗体，旨在抑制一种叫做 CGRP 的神经肽，降钙素基因相关肽的缩写。
仅在 1980 年代发现的 CGRP 是神经系统头部疼痛通路的关键组成部分，似乎与偏头痛和偏头痛的产生密切相关。科学家们现在认为，这些头痛的部分原因是与头部疼痛信号相关的大脑结构变得过度活跃——一种神经血管通讯出现问题。虽然大脑本身不会感到疼痛，但它可以通过将剧烈的疼痛感发送到头部的某些部位——很可能是颅骨和大脑之间称为脑膜的组织膜层——来应对这种错误的活动。发生这种情况时，患者对光、声音和气味的敏感性也会增加，以及恶心甚至呕吐。对于一些患有偏头痛的人来说，这种情况可能会持续数小时，有时甚至数天。这种新型药物，其中第一个由安进和诺华公司推向市场，统称为 CGRP 抑制剂，正在改变许多头痛患者的生活，可以毫不夸张地说，研究之间可以划清界限去年春天的那天，我获得了大脑奖和我厨房里的活动。我可以明确地说它对我有用吗？可能是。我认同。不久之后我的头痛就减轻了——尽管集群的特征是无论如何都会进入缓解期，然后又重新出现。但是，即使它确实奏效了——并且尽管这些进步受到欢迎——今天工作的任何神经学家都会欣然承认，就这些最常见和使人衰弱的疾病的许多方面而言，我们仍处于石器时代。这可能至少部分是历史偏见的结果。正如罗格斯大学社会学副教授乔安娜·肯普纳 (Joanna Kempner) 在 2014 年出版的《不是今晚：偏头痛与性别与健康政治》一书中灵活地阐述的那样，医疗和科学机构对偏头痛的考虑尤其不足。 Kempner 博士认为，这种驳回至少部分是因为偏头痛更常出现在女性身上，她们的身体疼痛通常归因于心理或情感原因。虽然这些诊断现在大多被医疗机构抛弃，但她认为头痛治疗中的性别偏见并没有。可以肯定的是，我们的大脑是深不可测的复杂器官，而且很难研究。但是，肯普纳博士确定的动力学几乎肯定有助于将头痛力学和治疗的科学无知延长数十年，并且对每个人来说都是如此。毕竟，Amgen/Novartis 抗体是美国批准的第一种专门预防偏头痛的药物。第一次——那是仅仅三年前。 （到目前为止，副作用通常看起来不常见且轻微，但长期情况仍有待观察。）礼来（Eli Lilly）仅在 2019 年获得了其用于治疗丛集性头痛的 CGRP 抑制剂版本的批准，新类别的 CGRP 药物仍在新兴。但自从药物监管出现以来，所有其他旨在预防这些可怕的头痛的药物——一系列β受体阻滞剂、抗癫痫药、抗抑郁药和其他药物——都被研究、开发和设计用于其他疾病。他们似乎只是帮助了一些头痛的副作用，所以医生只是让他们试一试。在我作为一名探索头痛科学现代演变的记者的工作中，我曾与许多患者交谈，他们认为无知，无论是临床还是文化，仍然存在。与许多其他疼痛疾病一样，黑人患者的头部疼痛得到正确诊断的可能性远低于白人。虽然世界卫生组织将头痛列为地球上导致残疾和生产力下降的主要原因，但我们对头痛的了解与许多其他疾病一样，偏向于富裕世界，导致对发展中国家的发病率和影响的研究大大不足.与此同时，这些新药虽然对某些人非常有效，但对其他人来说却是又一次令人心碎的失败，这表明更基本的神经触发或目标仍然难以捉摸。这些药物也不是治愈方法，许多患者必须继续定期服用它们——即使单次注射的成本可能超过 600 美元。许多头痛患者发现他们的保险公司拒绝为他们提供保险。尽管最近取得了所有进展，但美国国立卫生研究院为头痛疾病的基础研究提供的资金仍然与其巨大的社会成本不相称，而且很少有年轻医生将头痛专业视为职业发展的沃土。一位对神经病学感兴趣的年轻医学生告诉我，鉴于头痛是如此普遍——而且仍然如此神秘，她的研究如此专注于不寻常的脑部疾病，这让她大吃一惊。 “我们得到了很多关于其他事情的教导，这些事情也很重要，但不太常见，”她说。 “我对此感到很惊讶。”
看，我明白了。在一个被各种疾病和动荡困扰的世界里，抱怨头痛似乎很荒谬。但这也是让慢性头痛患者——或者任何患有任何类型慢性疼痛的人——成为独一无二的孤独者的原因。当然，疼痛不会杀死我们，但我们可以在任何时候接受长时间的身体殴打，而无需解释——一些研究表明，这种无情的循环会增加自杀风险。有些头痛甚至被认为能够产生人体已知的一种最不寻常的疼痛感觉，但它们发生的位置使迷惑的旁观者完全看不到它们（在历史的大部分时间里，科学家也是如此） .所有这些疼痛都有一个名字——“头痛”——如此普遍、如此乏味，而且被其他较轻的事物的共鸣彻底淡化，以至于患者常常羞于提及它。但是，问问任何患有偏头痛或类似头痛的人，他们有多少时间被无知的医生驳回或误诊，或者在吞咽、吸入或注射使他们昏昏欲睡或愚蠢的标签外药物的时间里，使他们的骨头变脆或心跳加速，或出现一连串的其他副作用。他们在你的家庭中。或者你和他们一起工作。不，他们不会死。但他们经常经历难以理解的、令人筋疲力尽的痛苦——或者生活在恐惧中，再次恐惧它就在眼前。 “除了身体上的疼痛之外，对我来说最痛苦的事情是偏头痛给我带来的难以置信的失落感，”一位当时只有 22 岁的年轻头痛患者告诉我。 “我知道我很聪明，但我的功课并没有反映出来。我知道我很社交，一个好女儿，一个好朋友，但我担心我的生活也不能反映这一点，”她说。 “我想我所感受到的损失是失去了在没有偏头痛的情况下我本来可以、可能或者可以做的事情。” 3 月，头痛科学的患者和倡导者再次向国会发出年度呼吁，要求为研究和其他头痛治疗计划提供额外资金。一年一度的世界偏头痛峰会为全球的患者和从业者提供在线访问研究前沿专家的机会。从德克萨斯州到丹麦，头痛研究中心正忙于研究这些疾病，更新药物的市场潜力不断增长，其他潜在疗法（从裸盖菇素到维生素 D）的临床试验正在进行中。头痛治疗兴趣的上升令人振奋且充满期待，而且正在进行的虚张声势的科学是非同寻常的。这确实是头痛的好时机。但同样令人沮丧的是，一种给这么多人带来如此频繁痛苦的疾病仍然如此神秘——而且治疗不足。 Tom Zeller Jr. (@tomzellerjr) 是纽约时报的前记者，是数字科学杂志 Undark 的主编。他正在写一本关于头痛的书。 《泰晤士报》致力于向编辑发表各种信函。我们想听听您对此或我们任何文章的看法。这里有一些提示。这是我们的电子邮件：letters@nytimes.com。
note_en:
I have headaches. Not the low-grade, annoying, “I’ve got a headache” sort of headaches. I get those, too. Most everyone does, and they are a drag.
 No, when I say that I get headaches, I mean that at intervals that are largely unpredictable, a knot of pain rises deep inside my head, invariably sensed behind my right eyeball. It then swiftly clicks up through the intensity scale, racing past that dull ache you might get from staring at the screen too long, leapfrogging over that doozy you had the morning after your brother’s wedding, skipping past the agonizing-but-fleeting stab of an ice-cream headache, and arriving, within a matter of minutes, at a pain so piercing and sustained that I can only grip something sturdy, rock back and forth, and grunt until it subsides.
 Mine are what doctors call one of the “primary headaches” — recurring and often excruciating disorders that are not byproducts of another condition (or self-inflicted by last night’s cocktails), but relentless, and in many ways still poorly understood disorders unto themselves. We know them by common names like migraine, which affects  tens of millions of Americans, disproportionately women. I suffer from another flavor known as cluster headaches (technically  “trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias”). And  there are others, with myriad and imperfectly drawn lines distinguishing them.
 If you experience migraines or cluster headaches — and research suggests that  more than a billion people worldwide do — you probably know something about shuttling from doctor to doctor looking for someone who “gets it.” You know what it’s like to gladly gobble up pills that don’t really work and that leave you miserable in other ways. And you might even know the same sort of incredulous exasperation that has driven me to wonder, from my fetal position on the bathroom floor: “How is it possible that science can’t fix a damn headache?”
 I won’t go so far as to say we’re anywhere near a fix. In many ways, we’re farther from understanding these disorders than we ought to be, given the billions of dollars they extract in health care costs, and the outsize toll they take on people — usually  during what ought to be their most productive and creative years. But I can also say with some confidence: There’s probably never been a better time to have a headache.
 In March, one of the most coveted prizes in neuroscience, the  $1.5 million Brain Prize, went to  four scientists who, often working independently of one another and beginning roughly in the 1980s, set about revolutionizing our understanding of migraines and similar headaches. Their work and that of many others has been slow but steady, overturning long-prevailing and facile notions like “the migraine personality” (a derisive category  primarily aimed at women), or the idea that stress and a weak constitution were the cause.
 They’re not. Rather, primary headaches are true neurobiological disorders and I know this because on a sunny day last spring, and after a few weeks of imperfectly managing excruciating pain with lousy drugs and haggling with doctors and insurance carriers, I stood in my kitchen, hiked down my trousers, and administered into my bottom three (still very expensive) injections of a new elixir — a monoclonal antibody designed to inhibit a neuropeptide called  CGRP  , short for   calcitonin gene-related peptide.
 CGRP, discovered only in the 1980s, is a key component in the nervous system’s head pain pathway and appears to be intimately involved in the generation of migraines and clusters. Scientists now believe these headaches arise in part when cerebral structures linked to head pain signaling become hyperactive — a sort of neurovascular communication gone awry. And while the brain itself does not feel pain, it can respond to this errant activity by sending severe sensations of pain to parts of the head that can — quite likely the membranous layers of tissue between the skull and the brain called the meninges. When this happens, patients can also experience increased sensitivity to light, sound and smell, as well as nausea and even vomiting. This can continue unabated for hours, and sometimes days, for some people living with migraine.
 This new class of medication, the  first of which was brought to market by Amgen and Novartis and collectively called CGRP inhibitors, is changing the lives of many headache patients, and it is no exaggeration to say that a direct line can be drawn between the research honored with the Brain Prize and events in my kitchen that day last spring. Can I say unequivocally that it worked for me? Maybe. I think so. My headaches abated shortly after — though it’s characteristic of clusters to go into periods of remission anyway, only to reappear.
 But even if it did work — and welcome as these advances are — any neurologist working today will readily admit that we remain in the Stone Age with regard to many aspects of these most common and debilitating disorders. That is probably the result, at least in part, to historical biases. As Joanna Kempner, an associate professor of sociology at Rutgers University, nimbly laid out in her 2014  book “Not Tonight: Migraine and the Politics of Gender and Health,” migraine headaches in particular have been given inadequate consideration by the medical and scientific establishments. This dismissal, Dr. Kempner argues, was at least partly tied to the fact that migraines surface more often in women, whose physical pain was often ascribed a psychological or emotional origin. And while those diagnoses have now mostly been discarded by the medical establishment, she suggests that gender biases in headache treatment have not.
  To be sure, our brains are unfathomably complex organs, and they are difficult to study. But the dynamics that Dr. Kempner identifies almost certainly helped to prolong scientific ignorance of headache mechanics and treatment for decades, and for everyone. Consider, after all, that the Amgen/Novartis antibody was the first drug ever approved in the United States to specifically prevent migraines. The  first — and that was a mere three years ago. (Side effects  so far generally appear uncommon and mild, though the long-term picture remains to be seen.) Eli Lilly secured approval for its version of a CGRP inhibitor to treat cluster headaches only  in 2019, and new classes of CGRP drugs are  still emerging. But since the dawn of drug regulation, every other pharmacological dollop aimed at preventing these diabolical headaches — a menagerie of beta blockers, anti-epileptics, anti-depressants and other drugs — were researched, developed and designed for other diseases. They simply seemed to help some people with headaches as a side effect, and so doctors just gave them a go.
 In my work as a journalist exploring the modern evolution in headache science, I’ve spoken to many patients who suggested that ignorance, both clinical and cultural, persists. As with so many other pain disorders, Black patients are  far less likely to have their head pain properly diagnosed than their white counterparts. And while the World Health Organization places headaches among the  leading causes of disability and lost productivity on the planet, our knowledge of headaches, like that of so many other diseases, skews toward the rich world, leaving incidence and impact in the developing world  vastly understudied.
 At the same time, the new drugs, while remarkably effective for some people, are yet another heartbreaking failure for others, suggesting that a more fundamental neural trigger or target remains elusive. These medications are also not cures, and many patients must continue to take them periodically — even though they can cost more than  $600 for a single injection. Many headache sufferers are finding that  their insurers will refuse to cover them.
 For all the recent progress, funding from the National Institutes of Health for basic research into headache disorders remains  incommensurate with their enormous social costs, and  far too few young doctors see headache specialization as fertile ground for a career. One young medical student with an interest in neurology told me she was taken aback that her studies focused so heavily on unusual brain disorders, given that headaches are so common — and still so mysterious. “We got so much teaching about other things, which are important too, but way less common,” she said. “I was surprised by that.”
 Look, I get it. In a world troubled by all manner of disease and unrest, it can seem absurd to complain about headaches. But this, too, is what makes the path of a chronic headache sufferer — or really anyone with any sort of chronic pain — a uniquely lonely one. The pain won’t kill us, sure, but we can receive prolonged physical beatings, without explanation, at any moment — a merciless cycle that some  studies have  shown can increase suicide risk. Some headaches are even thought to be capable of administering one of the  most extraordinary sensations of pain known to the human body, but they occur in a location that makes them entirely invisible to puzzled onlookers (and for most of history, to scientists, too). And all such pain bears a name — “headache” — so common, so boring and so utterly diluted by its resonance with other, lesser things, that sufferers are often too ashamed to even mention it.
 But ask any people who suffer from migraines or similar headaches about the months spent being dismissed or misdiagnosed by unenlightened doctors, or the years spent swallowing or inhaling or injecting hand-me-down, off-label drugs that make them sleepy or stupid, that embrittle their bones or race their hearts, or present a litany of other side effects. They are in your family. Or you work with them. No, they won’t die. But they are very often experiencing inscrutable, exhausting bouts of pain — or living in fear that it’s just around the corner, again.
 “The most painful thing to me, aside from my physical pain, is the incredible feeling of loss migraines have brought me,” a young headache patient, just 22 at the time, told me. “I know I am smart, but my schoolwork does not mirror it. I know I am social, a good daughter, a good friend, but I worry my life does not mirror this either,” she said. “I guess the loss I feel is the loss of what I could have been, or could be, or could do, without migraines.”
 In March, patients and advocates for headache science once again made an annual  appeal to Congress for additional funding for research and other headache treatment initiatives. The yearly  Migraine World Summit offers patients and practitioners around the globe online access to experts working at the bleeding edge of research. Headache research centers are busy studying these disorders from  Texas to  Denmark, there’s a  growing market potential in still newer drugs, and  clinical trials for other potential therapies — from  psilocybin to  vitamin D — are in the works.
 This upswing in therapeutic interest in headaches is exhilarating and full of anticipation, and the swashbuckling science being done is extraordinary. It really is a fine time to have a headache. But it’s also disheartening that a condition that brings such regular anguish to so many people remains so mysterious — and so undertreated.
  Tom Zeller Jr. ( @tomzellerjr), a former reporter for The New York Times, is the editor in chief of the digital science magazine  Undark. He is working on a book about headaches.

title: 头痛的最佳时机
title_en: There Has Never Been a Better Time to Have a Headache

note: 我不想再说一遍：尽可能地得到它。最近我尝试提交 iDOS 更新并修复了一些错误，但随后收到了 Apple 审核团队的通知，我的心凉了。为确保您的应用程序在 App Store 上的可用性不会中断，请在此消息发出之日起 14 天内提交更新。如果我们在 14 天内未收到符合 App Store 审核指南的更新，您的应用将被下架销售。我认为理解 Apple 背后的推理对所有人都有好处，所以我在最后包含了整个信息。长期以来的 iDOS 用户都知道自去年以来我们已经能够有意义地更新 iDOS，因为我们启用了文件共享访问，这使 iDOS 能够运行自定义游戏或程序。我们没有耍任何花招来愚弄审稿人，相反，对于任何提交，我们总是在他们面前提供以下说明：向可能担心的 js/python 应用程序开发人员道歉。我只是绝望了。也就是说，不知何故更新总是通过，并导致最近的流行，然后它可能在Apple内部引起了一些不利的关注。底线是我不能让自己削减 iDOS2 的关键功能以符合 Apple 的政策。对于专门为这些功能购买此应用程序的所有用户来说，这将是一种背叛。现有用户应该仍然可以在您购买的历史记录中下载此应用程序，但是，如果有一天您不能下载并且应用程序商店说“已被开发人员删除”，那绝对不是我做的。 2021 年 7 月 22 日晚上 11:15 来自 Apple 2. 5.2 - 应用程序应该是自包含的 请仔细查看此信息，因为它会影响您的应用程序在 App Store 上的可用性并需要您立即采取行动。您好，我们写信是为了让您了解有关您的应用程序 iDOS 2 版本 2.6.2 的新信息。重新评估后，我们发现您的应用不符合 App Store 审核指南。具体而言，我们发现您的应用违反了以下内容： 准则 2.5.2 - 性能 - 软件要求 在审核期间，您的应用安装或启动了 App Store 不允许的可执行代码。具体来说，您的应用程序执行 iDOS 包和图像文件，并允许 iTunes 文件共享和文件支持导入游戏。执行代码可以引入或更改应用程序的特性或功能，并允许在没有许可的情况下下载内容。请注意，虽然旨在教授、开发或允许学生测试可执行代码的教育应用程序可能会在有限的情况下下载代码，但此类代码不得用于其他目的，并且此类应用程序必须使源代码完全可由以下人员查看和编辑用户。后续步骤 - 查看 App Store 审核指南的软件要求部分。 - 确保您的应用符合 App Store 审核指南的所有部分以及 Apple Developer Program 的条款和条件。 - 一旦您的应用完全合规，请重新提交您的应用以供审核。为确保您的应用程序在 App Store 上的可用性不会中断，请在此消息发出之日起 14 天内提交更新。如果我们在 14 天内没有收到符合 App Store 审核指南的更新，您的应用将被下架销售。请注意，如果您的应用因任何原因被发现不合规并在提供的时间段过后被拒绝，您的应用将被下架，直到合规更新提交、批准并发布到 App Store。如果您的应用下架，该应用的 TestFlight 版本也将无法用于外部和内部测试，并且所有公共 TestFlight 链接将不再有效。故意无视 App Store 审核指南并试图欺骗用户或破坏审核流程是不可接受的，并且是直接违反 Apple 开发者计划许可协议第 3.2(f) 条的行为。继续违反 Apple Developer Program 的条款和条件将导致您的帐户以及任何相关或链接的帐户被终止，并从 App Store 中删除您的所有相关应用程序。此应用程序的未来提交可能需要更长的审核时间，并且此应用程序将不符合加急审核的条件。如果您对此信息有任何疑问，请回复此消息让我们知道。最好的问候， App Store 评论
note_en:
I hate to say it again:  Get it while you can.  Recently I tried to submit an iDOS update with a few bug fixes, but then got a notification from Apple Review Team, and my heart dropped cold.  To ensure there is no interruption of the availability of your app on the App Store, please submit an update within 14 days of the date of this message. If we do not receive an update compliant with the App Store Review Guidelines within 14 days, your app will be removed from sale.
 I think it's beneficial to all to understand Apple's reasoning behind this, so I have included the entire message at the end.  Long time iDOS users are aware that we have been able to update iDOS meaningfully since   last year , because we have enabled file sharing access which gives iDOS the ability to run custom games or programs. We didn't play any trick to fool the reviewers, on the contrary, for any submission, we always provide the following note up front to them:    Apology to js/python app devs who may be concerned. I was just being desperate. That said, somehow the updates always got through, and led to the   recent polularity, then it may have caught some unfavorable attention inside Apple.  The bottom line is that I can not bring myself to cut the critical functionalities of iDOS2 in order to be compliant with Apple's policy. That would be a betrayal to all the users that have purchased this app specifically for those features. Existing users should still be able to download this app in your purchased history, however, if someday you can't and the appstore says "removed by developer", it's definitely not my doing.    Jul 22, 2021 at 11:15 PM From Apple 2. 5.2 - Apps should be self-contained Please review this information carefully as it impacts your app’s availability on the App Store and requires your immediate action. Hello, We are writing to let you know about new information regarding your app, iDOS 2, version 2.6.2. Upon re-evaluation, we found that your app is not in compliance with the App Store Review Guidelines. Specifically, we found your app is in violation of the following: Guideline 2.5.2 - Performance - Software Requirements During review, your app installed or launched executable code, which is not permitted on the App Store. Specifically, your app executes iDOS package and image files and allows iTunes File Sharing and Files support for importing games. Executing code can introduce or changes features or functionality of the app and allows for downloading of content without licensing. Please note that while educational apps designed to teach, develop, or allow students to test executable code may, in limited circumstances, download code, such code may not be used for other purposes and such apps must make the source code completely viewable and editable by the user. Next Steps - Review the Software Requirements section of the App Store Review Guidelines. - Ensure your app is compliant with all sections of the App Store Review Guidelines and the Terms & Conditions of the Apple Developer Program. - Once your app is fully compliant, resubmit your app for review. To ensure there is no interruption of the availability of your app on the App Store, please submit an update within 14 days of the date of this message. If we do not receive an update compliant with the App Store Review Guidelines within 14 days, your app will be removed from sale. Please note, if your app is found to be out of compliance for any reason and rejected after the time period provided has elapsed, your app will be removed from sale until a compliant update is submitted, approved and released to the App Store. If your app is removed from sale, the TestFlight version of this app will also be unavailable for external and internal testing and all public TestFlight links will no longer be functional. Deliberate disregard of the App Store Review Guidelines and attempts to deceive users or undermine the review process are unacceptable and is a direct violation Section 3.2(f) of the Apple Developer Program License Agreement. Continuing to violate the Terms & Conditions of the Apple Developer Program will result in the termination of your account, as well as any related or linked accounts, and the removal of all your associated apps from the App Store. Future submissions of this app may require a longer review time, and this app will not be eligible for an expedited review. If you have any questions about this information, please reply to this message to let us know. Best regards, App Store Review
title: iDOS 2 即将消失

在我攻读博士学位的第一年，我开始了创伤康复治疗以从家庭暴力中康复。它主要由称为“再处理会话”的东西组成；使用一种叫做 EMDR 的技术，我们将重温我生活中的创伤记忆，并试图弄清楚我有哪些与这些事件相关的信念。我们所有人都带着对将要发生的事情和我们是谁的信念在世界上穿行。诸如“我不能相信任何人”之类的信念可能来自人际关系中的负面经历。 “我不够好”可能来自于多年来贬低自己并与他人比较。 “一切都糟透了”可能来自一切事物的悠久历史，嗯……糟透了。创伤康复治疗的目的是让我可以重写我的故事，向前迈进，而不是被我过去的经历所削弱。虽然我们过去的经历肯定会影响我们对世界的信念，但有时它们是无益的。如果我们看到不健康的人际关系模式，或者在学校被欺负，或者不得不成长得太快，或者过早失去某人，或者从未在媒体中看到自己：所有这些都是影响我们以后信仰的创伤形式线。想象一下，你可以收集你曾经拥有的每一次经历；你在镜子里对自己说的一切，你被告知的一切都是“正确”的生活方式。作为机器学习科学家，我们知道训练数据可以成就或破坏您的模型。你从哪里得到数据，它有多大的偏差，它是什么时候采样的，它是如何分类的。当我们试图建立一个可概括的世界模型时，我们会考虑这些问题中的每一个。那么为什么我们不对我们的个人历史应用同样的担忧呢？很多时候，我们对脑海中说我们不够好的声音给予了不成比例的重视。我们无意识地这样做，从来没有调查过数据来自哪里，也没有费心更新数据库。当我们在一生中吸收数据时，我们会在大量经验的基础上构建模型和启发式方法，以告知我们下一步该做什么。虽然我们当然已经学会了像不碰热炉这样的基本模型，但我们是否学会了如何增强自己的能力就不太清楚了。如何应对生活压力；如何与他人沟通；如何发现我们的真实身份。当我经历家庭暴力和虐待的痛苦经历时，我开始了关于改进机器学习教育的博士研究。一直激励着我的想法是，当我们被包含在数据中时，我们学得最好；当我们有一些与自己相关的东西时。当我们积极参与数据生成过程时，我们可能会更了解算法。所以我开始研究使用个人数据进行机器学习教育的主题。当我们探索我们自己与数据的联系，我们自己在算法系统中的经验时，我们会自动进行自我反思的过程。当我与其他人一起教他们算法如何影响我们时，我正在努力通过创伤恢复重新训练我自己的世界模型；虐待不是爱。我值得更好的。我现在很安全。我被允许收回我自己的力量。我目前正在编写一本名为 Life Lessons from Algorithms 的书，这是一本通过不同机器学习算法展示的个人弹性历史。每个机器学习概念都可以教会我们一些关于我们自己的康复的东西。 “垃圾进，垃圾出”告诉我们，不健康的关系会影响我们未来期望得到的待遇。 “没有免费的午餐”告诉我们，每个人的旅程都是不同的，对一个人有效的方法并不对每个人都有效。过度拟合的倾向告诉我们，我们需要广泛的经验、信仰、哲学和友谊才能真正教会我们关于世界的知识。集成方法告诉我们，有时两个头确实比一个更好。维度诅咒告诉我们，我们永远无法将所有事情都考虑在内，作为人类有一定程度的信仰。
例如，考虑梯度下降。当我真的陷入困境时，我有一个人生箴言：“做下一件正确的事”。这意味着当我因自我怀疑和不确定性而螺旋式上升时，我需要停止想象我面前的整个未来，而只需专注于我可以做的下一件事情。我们不可能知道将要发生的事情；我们只能采取下一步行动。我们心中有一个目标：找到一份新工作、坠入爱河、变得健康……或者永远转瞬即逝的“快乐”。我们所能做的就是努力朝着正确的方向迈出一步。对于那些不熟悉梯度下降的人来说，它是一种用于获得“最佳”结果（通常称为最小值）的优化算法。该算法从参数空间的初始点开始，并迭代地“向下”或朝着我们希望的最小值前进。从字面上看，它是做下一件正确的事情，直到它收敛到性能良好的参数集为止。就像在生活中一样，有时事情必须先变得更糟才能好起来。朝着正确方向迈出的一步可能会让我们陷入不再为我们服务的工作、关系或日常事务中。梯度下降（以及许多机器学习算法）对此有一个特殊的解决方案。每隔一段时间，算法会在一个全新的地方随机重新启动，以防万一有更好的东西，它无法通过简单地向下倾斜。在生活中，有时我们需要信仰的飞跃。有时我们需要从头再来，找到最适合我们的。我们的每一次旅程都是意外、幸运、支持、爱、失落、喜悦和一点点魔法的结合。我们所能做的就是保持头脑清醒，不断朝着梦想的方向前进，时不时地向未知领域迈进。希望一路走来，我们找到了自己。 Yim 是华盛顿大学的博士生，研究教授机器学习的创新方法。他们对社交媒体上广泛传播的算法素养特别感兴趣，目的是创建工具，使用户能够在算法持续造成伤害时为自己发声。他们是非二元的，是家庭暴力幸存者的倡导者，他们有幸患有自闭症！ Yim Lucky Register，“这都是训练数据：使用机器学习的经验教训重新训练你的思想”，梯度，2021 年。@article{register2021trainingdata, author = {Register, Yim Lucky}，title = {It's All Training Data: Using Lessons from Machine Learning to Retrain Your Mind}, journal = {The Gradient}, year = {2021}, howpublished = {\url{ https://thegradient.pub/its-all-training-data/} }, } 如果你喜欢这篇文章并想了解更多信息，请订阅 Gradient 并在 Twitter 上关注我们。
note_en:
In the first year of my PhD, I started trauma recovery therapy to heal from domestic violence. It mostly consisted of something called “reprocessing sessions''; using a technique called  EMDR we would revisit traumatic memories in my life, and try to figure out what beliefs I had that were linked to those events. All of us move through the world with beliefs about what will happen and who we are. Beliefs like “I can’t trust anyone” might come from negative experiences in relationships. “I’m not good enough” might come from years of putting yourself down and comparing yourself to others. “Everything sucks” likely comes from a long history of everything, well… sucking.
 The point of trauma recovery therapy was so that I could rewrite my story moving forwards, instead of being debilitated by my past experiences.
  While our past experiences certainly inform our beliefs about the world, sometimes they are unhelpful. If we saw unhealthy models of relationships, or were bullied in school, or had to grow up too fast, or lost someone too early, or never saw ourselves represented in the media: all of these are forms of trauma that affect our beliefs later down the line. Imagine you could collect every experience you’ve ever had; everything you’ve ever said to yourself in the mirror, everything you’ve ever been told was the “right” way to live your life.
  As machine learning scientists, we know that training data can make or break your model. Where you got the data from, how biased it is,  when it was sampled, how it was categorized. We think about each of these questions when trying to make a generalizable model of the world. So why don’t we apply the same concerns to our personal histories? So often we give disproportionate weight to the voice in our head that says we aren’t good enough. We do this unconsciously, without ever investigating where the data came from, or bothering to update the database.
 As we take in data throughout our lives, we build models and heuristics on top of our massive collection of experiences in order to inform us of what to do next. While we certainly have learned basic models like to not touch a hot stove, it’s less clear if we have learned how to empower ourselves; how to cope with life’s stresses; how to communicate with others; how to discover who we really are.
 As I worked through painful experiences of domestic violence and abuse, I was beginning my PhD research on improving Machine Learning education. The thoughts that kept inspiring me were that we learn best when we are included in the data; when we have something to relate back to ourselves. When we are an active participant in the data generating process, we might understand the algorithms more. So I began research on the topic of  using personal data for machine learning education.  When we explore our own connections to data, our own experiences within algorithmic systems, we automatically engage in a process of self-reflection. As I worked with others to teach them about how algorithms affect us, I was working on retraining my own models of the world through trauma recovery; Abuse was not love. I deserved better. I am safe now. And I am allowed to reclaim my own power.
 I’m currently working on a book called  Life Lessons from Algorithms , a personal history of resilience demonstrated through different machine learning algorithms. Each machine learning concept can teach us something about our own healing.  “Garbage in, garbage out” teaches us that unhealthy relationships affect how we expect to be treated in the future.  “No free lunch” teaches us that everyone’s journey is different, and what works for one person won’t work for everyone.  The tendency to overfit teaches us that we need a wide range of experiences, beliefs, philosophies, and friendships to truly teach us about the world.  Ensemble methods teach us that sometimes two heads really are better than one.  The curse of dimensionality teaches us that we can never take everything into account, that there is a certain level of faith inherent in being human.
 Consider, for example, gradient descent. I have a life mantra for when I’m really stuck:  “Do the next right thing”. It means that when I’m spiraling with self-doubt and uncertainty, I need to stop imagining a whole future in front of me and simply focus on the next thing that I can do. We cannot possibly know the landscape of what will happen; we can only take the next step. We have a goal in mind: to get a new job, to fall in love, to get healthy … or the ever-fleeting  “to be happy”. All we can do is try to take a step in the right direction.
  For those unfamiliar with gradient descent, it is an optimization algorithm used to get the “best” outcome (often referred to as a minima). The algorithm begins at an initial point in the parameter space, and iteratively takes steps “downward”, or towards what we hope is the minima. It is quite literally,  doing the next right thing until it converges on the set of parameters that perform well. Just as in life, sometimes things have to get worse before they get better. A step in what seemed like the right direction can leave us stuck in a job, a relationship, or a routine that no longer serves us. Gradient descent (and many machine learning algorithms) have a special solution to this. Every so often, the algorithm randomly restarts in an entirely new place,  just in case there’s something better it couldn’t get to by simply following the slope downwards. Sometimes in life, we need to take a leap of faith. Sometimes we need to start all over to find what’s best for us. Each of our journeys is a combination of accidents, luck, support, love, loss, joy, and a little bit of magic. All we can do to keep our head above water is to keep moving in the direction of our dreams, and every so often taking a leap into the unknown. The hope is that along the way, we find ourselves.
   Yim is a PhD student at the University of Washington, researching innovative ways to teach machine learning. They are particularly interested in widespread algorithmic literacy on social media, with the goal of creating tools to empower users to speak up for themselves when algorithms perpetuate harm. They are nonbinary,  an advocate for domestic violence survivors, and they are blessed with autism!
   Yim Lucky Register, "It’s All Training Data: Using Lessons from Machine Learning to Retrain Your Mind", The Gradient, 2021.

奥迪的 DTM 计划——就像德国的 NASCAR 一样——在 2020 年底结束，2021 年是这家德国汽车制造商在电动方程式中的最后一年。但奥迪运动在这两个项目中的投资不会白费，因为每台机器都组合在一起，为一台外观令人发指的机器提供动力，该机器旨在参加达喀尔拉力赛。今天在网上发布，这款车被称为奥迪 RS Q e-tron，奥迪表示，在世界上最艰难的越野比赛之一中进行竞选将使该公司能够“在极端条件下”测试和开发其电动动力系统。尽管在过去的几十年里，奥迪的赛车运动主要发生在赛道的范围内，但该品牌与拉力赛有着密切的联系。它利用 1980 年代的世界拉力锦标赛来展示其 quattro 全轮驱动技术。它们可能都涉及拉力赛，但达喀尔比 WRC 更具挑战性，因为它在偏远地区进行了为期两周的比赛，每天的距离为 500 英里（800 公里）。 “这是一个很长的距离，”奥迪运动达喀尔项目负责人 Andreas Roos 说。 “我们尝试做的事情以前从未有人做过。这是电动传动系统的终极挑战。”达喀尔的性质排除了纯粹的电池电动方法，尽管过去曾尝试过这种方法。相反，奥迪运动采用了系列混合动力方法——是的，系列混合动力车迷，我们认为这个方法通过了纯度测试。管架式 RS Q e-tron 使用来自 Audi Sport 当前电动方程式计划的一对 250 kW (335 hp) 电动机/发电机组 (MGU)，每个轴上安装一个。这些由 52 kWh（可用）锂离子电池组供电，该电池组可能是从奥迪 e-tron FE07 电动方程式赛车中借来的。总输出功率为 500 千瓦（670 马力）。然后是第三个 Formula E MGU，它是能量转换器的一部分，在 RS Q e-tron 行驶时为电池充电。 （两个推进 MGU 还将在制动时再生能量。）能量转换器的另一个组件是来自 Audi Sport 的 DTM 汽车的 2.0 L TSFI 发动机，它通过轴连接到第三个 MGU。您可能不会期望高度串联的赛车发动机可以作为发电机很好地工作，但奥迪运动表示，在正确的转速范围内，发动机实际上是高效的。 “我们将输出功率从 450 kW 降低到约 200 kW。它在 4,500 到 6,000 rpm 之间的狭窄优化转速范围内运行，而不是 9,000 rpm，”奥迪运动赛车项目开发负责人 Stefan Dreyer 说。 “发动机必须在炎热、寒冷和高海拔地区尽可能高效地运行。我们为此优化了活塞、喷油器和涡轮增压器，并在测试台上花费了大量时间。我们为特定消耗量感到自豪远低于每千瓦时 200 克。”
“传动系统非常高效。几乎没有任何功率损失。当你在沙地上缓慢行驶时，你不必担心温度问题。但最重要的是，在所有给定的情况下，电动传动系统更容易控制情况，”德雷尔说。 “从零转即可获得最大扭矩。您可以非常精确地调节动力传动系统，无论汽车是跳跃还是在沙漠中行驶。电动动力传动系统不会对气压、温度和湿度等外部影响做出反应。你可以在整个拉力赛中全功率使用它。它比内燃机更稳定。” RS Q e-tron 也将每晚充电。尽管奥迪运动还没有展示充电解决方案，但它表示计划使用尽可能多的清洁能源。并且这辆车的设计目的是由一个小型支持团队提供高度的维修服务。原型 RS Q e-tron 于 7 月初首次亮相，现在它正在接受今年剩余时间的广泛测试计划，然后是明年 1 月在沙特阿拉伯举行的 2022 年达喀尔拉力赛。奥迪运动将参加 Q Motorsport 运营的三辆 RS Q e-tron。 Mattias Ekström（为奥迪赢得 DTM 和 Rallycross 锦标赛冠军）和 Emil Bergkvist 将共享一辆赛车，一辆由 14 次达喀尔冠军 Stéphane Peterhansel 和 Edouard Boulanger 共享，第三辆由 Carlos Sainz（WRC 两项冠军和三项冠军）共享。时间达喀尔冠军）和卢卡斯克鲁兹。
title: 奥迪 RS Q e-tron 结合了三台电动方程式发动机和一台 DTM 发动机
title_en: Three Formula E motors and a DTM engine combine in the Audi RS Q e-tron

note: 非营利性消息传出后，新东方在香港上市的股票在盘后交易中下跌 44.22%，而好未来在纽约证券交易所的股票在盘前交易中跌幅更大，为 51.75%。随着雅虎财经在 TAL 即将在开盘时下跌之前列出了大约 138 亿美元的市值，数十亿的股票价值即将被删除。名单还在继续：例如，中国在线教育集团在盘后交易中下跌了 39.97%。中国政府对国内经济的一个部门施加更多控制的新决定应该不足为奇。我们不应该对在线辅导成为该国的目标感到震惊；今天的新闻是对今年早些时候该行业先前监管行动的跟进。近年来，随着中国成为教育科技初创公司的代名词，这一消息影响的不仅仅是上市公司。预期的规则变化也可能影响到许多私营的、风险投资支持的公司。比如，元复道会怎样？该公司去年估值 155 亿美元，提供 TechCrunch 所说的“现场辅导、在线问答部门和数学问题检查部门”。公司会看到它的翅膀被剪掉吗？或者去年单轮融资 16 亿美元的左业帮怎么样？ TechCrunch 写道，佐业帮“为幼儿园到 12 年级的学生提供在线课程、现场课程和作业帮助。”是不是也有问题？所有这一切都发生在 Zomato 的股票开始浮动的同一天，这家印度在线食品配送公司的股价在首日交易中上涨了近 65%。 TechCrunch 将 Zomato 的 IPO 视为更大的印度创业市场可能的风向标，结果对于该国其他专注于增长、亏损的独角兽来说是个好兆头。
note_en:
New Oriental’s Hong Kong-listed shares fell 44.22% in after-hours trading after the nonprofit news broke, while NYSE-shares of TAL are off an even sharper 51.75% in pre-market trading. With Yahoo Finance listing a roughly $13.8 billion market cap for TAL ahead of its impending declines at the market open, billions of equity value are about to get deleted. The list goes on: China Online Education Group is off 39.97% in after-hours trading, for example.
     A new decision by China’s government to exert more control over a sector of its domestic economy should not surprise. And we shouldn’t be shocked that online tutoring is in the country’s targets; today’s news is a follow-up to  prior regulatory action in the sector from earlier in the year.
 As China has become synonymous with edtech startups in recent years, the news impacts more than just public companies. The expected rules change may also hit a host of private, venture-backed companies.
 For example, what will happen to Yuanfudao? The company was  valued at $15.5 billion last year, offering what TechCrunch described as “live tutoring, an online Q&A arm and a math-problem-checking arm.” Will the company see its wings clipped?
 Or how about Zuoyebang, which  raised $1.6 billion in a single round last year? TechCrunch wrote that Zuoyebang offers “online courses, live lessons and homework help for kindergarten to 12th grade students.” Is it in trouble as well?
 All this comes on the same day that shares in Zomato began to float, with the Indian online food delivery company seeing its shares  close up nearly 65% in their first day’s trading. TechCrunch has viewed the Zomato IPO as a possible bellwether for the larger Indian startup market, and the results augur well for other growth-focused, loss-making unicorns in the country.
title: 中国预期的教育科技打压可能会令一个关键的初创行业寒心
title_en: China’s expected edtech clampdown may chill a key startup sector

一项新的民意调查显示，大多数尚未接种 COVID-19 疫苗的美国人表示，他们不太可能接种疫苗，并怀疑他们是否会对抗侵略性的 delta 变种，尽管他们确实这样做了一些州的感染。根据美联社-NORC 公共事务研究中心的一项民意调查，在尚未接种疫苗的美国成年人中，35% 的人表示他们可能不会，45% 的人表示肯定不会。只有 3% 的人表示他们肯定会中枪，而另外 16% 的人表示他们可能会。更重要的是，64% 的未接种疫苗的美国人几乎没有信心注射疫苗对变种有效——包括官员所说的导致美国 83% 新病例的 delta 变种——尽管有证据表明它们提供了强有力的保护。相比之下，已经接种疫苗的人中有 86% 至少对疫苗会起作用有一定的信心。这意味着“将会有更多可预防的病例、更多可预防的住院治疗和更多可预防的死亡，”约翰霍普金斯大学传染病专家 Amesh Adalja 博士说。 “我们一直都知道，无论数据显示什么，一定比例的人口都难以说服，（并且）很多人无法说服，”阿达利亚说。他赞同疾病控制和预防中心主任罗谢尔·瓦伦斯基 (Rochelle Walensky)，称当前的激增是“未接种疫苗的流行病”，因为几乎所有入院和死亡病例都是未接种疫苗的人。 AP-NORC 的调查是在本周几位共和党人和保守的有线新闻人物之前进行的，他们敦促人们在经过几个月的犹豫后接种疫苗。过去两周，美国的 COVID-19 病例几乎翻了三倍。根据疾病预防控制中心的数据，在全国范围内，包括儿童在内的所有美国人中有 56.4% 至少接种了一剂疫苗。白宫官员周四表示，在一些接种率落后且 COVID-19 病例增加的州，疫苗接种开始增加，包括阿肯色州、佛罗里达州、路易斯安那州、密苏里州和内华达州。
尽管如此，路易斯安那州仍有超过 40% 的人口至少接种了一剂，该州周三报告了 5,388 例新的 COVID-19 病例，这是自大流行开始以来第三高的单日数字。上个月住院人数也急剧上升。 AP-NORC 的调查发现，大多数美国人（54%）至少在某种程度上担心他们或他们的家人会被感染，其中 27% 的人非常担心。这比一个月前略有上升，但远低于今年年初，当时大约十分之七的美国人表示他们至少有点担心他们或他们认识的人会被感染。民主党人比共和党人更有可能说他们至少有点担心他们身边的人被感染，70% 到 38%。总体而言，共和党人比民主党人更有可能说他们没有接种疫苗，而且肯定或可能不会接种疫苗，比例为 43% 到 10%。观点也按年龄和教育程度划分：45 岁以下的人中有 37% 表示他们没有并且可能不会得到注射，而年长者中只有 16%。那些没有大学学位的人比有大学学位的人更有可能说他们没有也不会接种疫苗，比例为 30% 到 18%。住在佛罗里达州奥兰多附近的科迪·约翰森认为自己是一名保守的共和党人，但表示这与他跳过疫苗接种的决定无关。 26 岁在军事基地安装视听设备的 Johansen 说：“这对我的人口中的人来说并没有那么危险，而且我的免疫系统很好。” “我的大多数朋友都接种了疫苗，他们对我没有接种疫苗感到有些生气。有同辈压力，因为他们说这是公民责任。”他说，很明显这些注射剂是有效的，但让他有点困扰的是，它们只有食品和药物管理局的紧急使用授权。
约翰森说，他赞同乔拜登总统应对大流行的方式，并表示他表现出良好的领导能力。这反映了民意调查的结果。绝大多数美国人（66%）继续支持拜登应对疫情的方式——高于拜登 59% 的总体支持率。这种差异主要是由共和党人推动的，其中 32% 的人表示他们赞成拜登对 COVID-19 的处理，而总体上赞成他的人为 15%。大约十分之九的民主党人总体上赞成拜登以及他对大流行的处理。居住在伊利诺伊州罗克福德附近的航空航天工程师杰西·麦克马斯特 (Jessie McMasters) 说，在与助产士交谈并阅读了辉瑞 (Pfizer) 和 Moderna 疫苗的开发过程后，她在怀孕 37 周时接种了第一针疫苗。 “这让我对他们的工作充满信心，”麦克马斯特说。她的父母都被感染了，但没有患上重病，后来都接种了疫苗。她说，当谈到他们对疫苗接种和其他病毒预防措施的看法时，她的朋友和家人到处都是——这通常反映了此类讨论如何变得具有党派色彩。支持民主党的麦克马斯特斯说，一些获得它的人“在某一方面可能永远不会放弃口罩，因为现在这是一项个人陈述”，而其他人则因为他们的政治信仰或错误信息而不会得到注射。哈佛陈公共卫生学院教授 Howard Koh 博士说，疫苗犹豫并不新鲜，但围绕 COVID-19 和快速传播的变异的错误信息使得一对一地接触人们以了解他们的情况势在必行。关注并提供准确的信息。
“我从我的病人身上学到的是，当亲人去世时，那是一场悲剧，”奥巴马政府高级公共卫生官员 Koh 说。 “但是，当您所爱的人去世并且您知道它可以避免时，那场悲剧将永远困扰着您。” AP-NORC 于 7 月 15 日至 19 日对 1,308 名成年人进行了民意调查，使用的样本来自 NORC 基于概率的 AmeriSpeak 小组，该小组旨在代表美国人口。所有受访者的抽样误差幅度为正负 3.7 个百分点。
note_en:
Most Americans who haven’t been vaccinated against COVID-19 say they are unlikely to get the shots and doubt they would work against the aggressive delta variant despite evidence they do, according to a new poll that underscores the challenges facing public health officials amid soaring infections in some states.
 Among American adults who have not yet received a vaccine, 35% say they probably will not, and 45% say they definitely will not, according to a poll from The Associated Press-NORC Center for Public Affairs Research. Just 3% say they definitely will get the shots, though another 16% say they probably will.
 What’s more, 64% of unvaccinated Americans have little to no confidence the shots are effective against variants — including the delta variant that officials say is responsible for 83% of new cases in the U.S. —  despite evidence that they offer strong protection. In contrast, 86% of those who have already been vaccinated have at least some confidence that the vaccines will work.
  That means “that there will be more preventable cases, more preventable hospitalizations and more preventable deaths,” said Dr. Amesh Adalja, an infectious disease specialist at Johns Hopkins University.
  “We always knew some proportion of the population would be difficult to persuade no matter what the data showed, (and) a lot of people are beyond persuasion,” said Adalja. He echoed Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Director Rochelle Walensky in calling the current surge  “a pandemic of the unvaccinated” because nearly all hospital admissions and deaths have been among those who weren’t immunized.
 The AP-NORC survey was conducted before several Republicans and conservative cable news personalities this week  urged people to get vaccinated after months of stoking hesitancy. That effort comes  as COVID-19 cases nearly tripled in the U.S. over the past two weeks.
 Nationally, 56.4% of all Americans, including children, have received at least one dose of the vaccine, according to the CDC. And White House officials said Thursday that vaccinations are beginning to increase in some states where rates are lagging behind and COVID-19 cases are rising, including in Arkansas, Florida, Louisiana, Missouri and Nevada.
  Still, just over 40% of Louisiana’s population has received at least one dose, and the state reported 5,388 new COVID-19 cases Wednesday — the third-highest single-day figure since the pandemic began. Hospitalizations also rose steeply in the last month.
 The AP-NORC survey found that the majority of Americans — 54% — are at least somewhat concerned that they or someone in their family will be infected, including 27% who are very concerned. That’s up slightly from a month ago, but far below the beginning of the year, when about 7 in 10 Americans said they were at least somewhat concerned that they or someone they knew would be infected.
 Democrats are far more likely than Republicans to say they’re at least somewhat concerned about someone close to them being infected, 70% to 38%.
 And overall, Republicans are far more likely than Democrats to say they have not been vaccinated and definitely or probably won’t be, 43% to 10%. Views are also divided along age and education lines: Thirty-seven percent of those under age 45 say they haven’t and likely won’t get the shots, compared with just 16% of those older. And those without college degrees are more likely than those with them to say they aren’t and won’t be vaccinated, 30% to 18%.
 Cody Johansen, who lives near Orlando, Florida, considers himself a conservative Republican, but said that had no bearing on his decision to skip vaccination.
 “It hasn’t really been that dangerous to people in my demographic, and I have a good immune system,” said Johansen, a 26-year-old who installs audio-visual equipment at military bases. “Most of my friends got vaccinated, and they’re a little mad at me for not getting it. There is peer pressure because they say it’s a civic responsibility.”
 He said it’s obvious the shots have been effective, though it bothers him a little that they have only emergency use authorization from the Food and Drug Administration.
 Johansen said he approves of how President Joe Biden has handled the pandemic response, saying he has exhibited good leadership.
 That reflects the poll’s findings. A large majority of Americans, 66%, continue to approve of how Biden is handling the pandemic — higher than Biden’s overall approval rating of 59%.
 The difference is fueled largely by Republicans, 32% of whom say they approve of Biden’s handling of COVID-19 compared with 15% who approve of him overall. About 9 in 10 Democrats approve of Biden overall and for his handling of the pandemic.
 Jessie McMasters, an aerospace engineer who lives near Rockford, Illinois, said she got her first shot when she was 37 weeks pregnant after talking with her midwife and reading about how the Pfizer and Moderna vaccines were developed.
 “That gave me high confidence that they worked,” said McMasters. Her parents both were infected but did not suffer serious illness, and both have since been vaccinated.
 She said her friends and family are all over the place when it comes to their views on vaccination and other virus-prevention measures — often reflecting how such discussions have become partisan. Some who got it are “so far on one end that they may never give up masks because now it’s a personal statement,” said McMasters, who leans Democratic, just as others won’t get the shots because of their political beliefs or misinformation.
 Dr. Howard Koh, a professor at the Harvard Chan School of Public Health, said vaccine hesitancy is not new, but  the misinformation surrounding COVID-19 and the fast-spreading variant make it imperative to reach people one-on-one to understand their concerns and provide accurate information.
  “What I learned from my patients is that when a loved one dies, that’s a tragedy,” said Koh, who was a senior public health official in the Obama administration. “But when a loved one dies and you know it could have been prevented, that tragedy haunts you forever.”
    The AP-NORC poll of 1,308 adults was conducted July 15-19 using a sample drawn from NORC’s probability-based AmeriSpeak Panel, which is designed to be representative of the U.S. population. The margin of sampling error for all respondents is plus or minus 3.7 percentage points.
title: 大多数未接种疫苗的美国人不想注射：AP-NORC 民意调查
title_en: Most Unvaccinated Americans Don't Want Shots: AP-NORC Poll

note: Apple 的 Pro Display XDR 已经上市有一段时间了，但目前还没有关于其高价显示器更新版本的传言。然而，9to5Mac 现在从熟悉此事的消息人士那里获悉，苹果正在内部测试一款带有专用 A13 芯片和神经引擎的新外部显示器。新显示器的开发代号为 J327，但目前尚不清楚有关技术规格的详细信息。据消息人士透露，这款显示器将配备苹果制造的 SoC，目前是 A13 仿生芯片——与 iPhone 11 系列中使用的芯片相同。与 A13 芯片一起，外部显示器具有神经引擎，可加速机器学习任务。尽管有传言称苹果一直在为普通用户开发更便宜的显示器，但带有专用 SoC 的新外部显示器将来很可能会成为替代当前 Pro Display XDR 的新型号。早在 2016 年，就在 Apple 的 Thunderbolt Display 停产之后，有传言称 Apple 正在研究更换高分辨率显示器和内置 GPU。虽然该公司确实在 2019 年推出了自己的高级外接显示器，但 Pro Display XDR 并没有像传言那样配备 GPU。今天来自 9to5Mac 的消息来源的报告表明，苹果并没有放弃其推出带有某种 SoC 的外部显示器的计划，至少在内部是这样。在外部显示器中内置 CPU/GPU 可以帮助 Mac 提供高分辨率图形，而无需使用计算机内部芯片的所有资源。 Apple 还可以将显示 SoC 的强大功能与 Mac 的 SoC 结合起来，为运行密集的图形任务提供更高的性能。请记住，Apple 的计划可能会发生变化，就像第一款 Pro Display XDR 从未拥有传闻中的所有功能一样。苹果对这款新外置显示器的发布计划也不得而知。您正在阅读 9to5Mac — 日复一日地发布有关 Apple 及其周边生态系统新闻的专家。请务必查看我们的主页以获取所有最新消息，并在 Twitter、Facebook 和 LinkedIn 上关注 9to5Mac 以了解最新消息。不知道从哪里开始？查看我们的独家故事、评论、操作方法，并订阅我们的 YouTube 频道
note_en:
Apple’s  Pro Display XDR has been available for quite some time now, but there are still no rumors about an updated version of its high-priced display in the near term. However,  9to5Mac has now learned from sources familiar with the matter that Apple is internally testing a new external display with a dedicated A13 chip and also Neural Engine.
  The new display is being developed under the codename J327, but at this point, details about technical specifications are unclear. According to sources, this display will have an Apple-made SoC, which right now is the A13 Bionic chip — the same one used in the iPhone 11 lineup.
 Along with the A13 chip, the external display features the Neural Engine, which accelerates machine learning tasks. Although rumors suggest that Apple has been working on a  less expensive display for regular users, the new external display with a dedicated SoC will likely be a new model to replace the current Pro Display XDR in the future.
 Back in 2016, right after Apple’s Thunderbolt Display was discontinued,  rumors suggested that Apple was working on a replacement with a high-resolution display and a built-in GPU. While the company has indeed launched its own premium external display in 2019, Pro Display XDR does not come with a GPU as rumors predicted.
 Today’s report from  9to5Mac’s sources suggests that Apple has not given up on its plans to launch an external display with some sort of SoC, at least internally. Having a CPU/GPU built into the external display could help Macs deliver high-resolution graphics without using all the resources of the computer’s internal chip. Apple could also combine the power of the display SoC with the Mac’s SoC to provide even more performance for running intensive graphic tasks.
 Keep in mind that Apple’s plans may change, just as the first Pro Display XDR never had all the features it was rumored to have. Apple’s launch plans for this new external display are also unknown.
  You’re reading 9to5Mac — experts who break news about Apple and its surrounding ecosystem, day after day. Be sure to check out  our homepage for all the latest news, and follow 9to5Mac on  Twitter,  Facebook, and  LinkedIn to stay in the loop. Don’t know where to start? Check out our  exclusive stories,  reviews,  how-tos, and  subscribe to our YouTube channel

Facebook 告诉伊朗活动人士，它将允许人们在有限的两周时间内发布“哈梅内伊之死”字样或播放人们说或念诵这句话的视频，这是一个奇怪的选择，突出了 Facebook 的力量，而且内容常常令人困惑。节制规则。在过去的几天里，胡齐斯坦西南地区的伊朗人因缺水而走上街头，在德黑兰引发了反政府抗议，第五波 COVID-19 和美国制裁导致的经济斗争也助长了这一点。伊朗政府一如以往多次以暴力回应，造成数人死亡。伊朗人一直在转向 Instagram 等社交媒体平台来记录骚乱。但是，一些流行的 Instagram 帐户，例如在 2019 年 11 月抗议活动造成死亡人数后自称为 1500tasvir（或“1500images”）的小组，已经删除了一些包含最近抗议活动视频的帖子。一组互联网审查研究人员和活动家已将这些删除标记到 Facebook 旗下的 Instagram。该公司的回应是，这些帖子被正确删除，因为它们包含呼吁“杀死哈梅内伊”，即该国的统治者阿里哈梅内伊。该公司在 Motherboard 查看的一封电子邮件中辩称，此类口号违反了 Facebook 关于煽动暴力的社区标准。伊朗审查制度研究人员兼专家马萨·阿里马尔达尼 (Mahsa Alimardani) 与专注于自由的非营利组织 ARTICLE19 合作表示，“哈梅内伊之死”是表达对伊朗政权不满的一种极为常见的方式，尤其是在抗议活动中。世界各地的表达，以及被撤职的活动家之一。在伊朗的背景下，它更像是高呼“操特朗普”之类的东西。 “我们认识到，在当前抗议缺水的背景下，‘哈梅内伊’在这些声明中主要被用作伊朗政权的替身，而不是直接威胁或呼吁对他个人施加暴力， ” 一位公司代表在一封电子邮件中告诉活动人士，Motherboard 查看了该邮件。 “因此，我们制定了一个有限的、有时限的新闻价值例外，以允许此内容。在接下来的两周内（根据当地情况进行进一步审查），我们将允许在“哈梅内伊之死”中使用“哈梅内伊之死”。伊朗政治抗议的背景。”该代表还补充说，该公司过去对“哈马内伊之死”的颂歌也有同样的例外，但没有说明具体时间。
在给 Motherboard 的一份声明中，Facebook 的一位发言人表示，“我们知道 #deathtokhamenei 这句话被用作批评和抗议政府的代理，而不是作为一种暴力威胁，并恢复了最初被删除的帖子。”换句话说，Facebook 知道这首颂歌并不是要杀死哈梅内伊的字面上的呼吁，并且之前已经例外。然而，它在过去几天中删除了几个帖子，现在将暂时允许人们说它，但前提是该国严重缺水。 “他们正在从伊朗删除抗议镜头，因为抗议者在说‘哈梅内伊之死’。”几乎在伊朗的每一次抗议活动中，都会提到‘独裁者之死’，”阿里马尔达尼告诉主板。 “我想不出比这更清楚地说明缺乏上下文理解的例子了。” Alimardani 说她向 1500tasvir 和其他讲波斯语的用户发送了一份表格，询问他们是否目睹了任何删除。截至周四，她说她已经收集了 249 条回复，其中 227 条报告说帖子被删除，有些包括颂歌，有些没有。然而，在帖子被删除并且现场和网上的活动人士指出错误之前，Facebook 发言人没有回答关于该公司之前何时做出这一例外的问题，也没有回答为什么这次没有做出例外。一位要求匿名以避免政府报复的 1500tasvir 激进组织的参与者告诉主板，“人们确实认为哈梅内伊作为最高领导人应对这些杀戮和枪击事件负责。他的角色非常明确，必须追究他的责任.” “Facebook 和 Instagram 将人们的口号和语言视为死亡威胁，必须重新考虑并永久改变，而不仅仅是两周，在这些平台的规则中考虑上下文和语言概念的多样性非常重要，”他们补充道。 .
Alimardani 提到了最近在以色列发生的事件，一些民族主义右翼分子高呼“阿拉伯人的死亡”，并说有几篇帖子记录了 Facebook 上的颂歌和事件。据她说，该公司对这些颂歌的回应，以及伊朗的颂歌似乎相互矛盾且令人困惑。 “在这种情况下，我们有一个集体试图记录抗议的场景，比如 1500tasvir，他们只是发布视频，当有数百人的人群时，他们显然无法控制，你无法控制每个人说。那么你怎么确定那是有新闻价值的？当[半岛电视台]发布“阿拉伯人之死”视频时，没关系，它具有新闻价值。但是当一个公民媒体集体发布一个视频说“哈梅内伊之死”时，他们决定了新闻价值吗？”电子前沿基金会国际言论自由主任、近期《硅价值观》的作者吉利安·约克表示，这是 Facebook 不知道如何处理与政治相关的言论自由问题的又一个例子，而不仅仅是在伊朗。 “这就是为什么我们首先有关于言论自由的价值观，因为谁或什么权威来决定什么是'death to'的适当用法？”约克告诉主板。“虽然希望某人死亡不一定是件好事，但同时，国家在大多数这些地方拥有最终权力，人们除了吟唱之外还有其他选择吗？”与此同时，如果你不喜欢伊朗的专制政权，请尽可能地加入你的颂歌或标签。
